question: is composting recycling
title: Is Composting Recycling? Why Organic Matter Needs Its Own Kind of Return
metaDescription: Composting is a form of recycling, but it's more than that. Scotty explains Albert Howard's Law of Return and why cycling organic matter back to soil is the foundation of sustainable agriculture.
content:
Technically, yes. Composting is a form of recycling. The EPA classifies it as organics recycling, the collection and processing of organic materials that would otherwise end up in a landfill, converted into a useful product.

But I want to push on that framing a little bit, because calling composting "recycling" undersells it. Recycling, in the way most people understand it, is about recovering material and reprocessing it into something of equivalent value. You take an aluminum can, melt it down, make a new can. Same material, same use.

Composting is different. It's not just recovery, it's transformation and return. And the destination is the most important part.

## Albert Howard and the Law of Return

Albert Howard was a British botanist and agricultural scientist working in India in the early 20th century. He spent decades studying traditional Indian farming practices and indigenous composting methods, and what he observed changed how he thought about the entire relationship between civilization and soil.

Howard wrote about what he called the Law of Return: whatever organic matter you remove from the land through crops, you must return to maintain soil fertility. He was describing a cycle, a closed loop. Food grows in soil, humans eat food, organic waste is returned to soil, soil produces more food. Round and round.

He saw Western industrial agriculture breaking that loop. Organic waste, human waste, food scraps, crop residues, was being treated as refuse, something to be disposed of. Sent to landfills, burned, flushed into rivers. The nutrient capital that came from the land was not going back.

The result, Howard argued, was the gradual mining of soil fertility. You can keep extracting without returning for a while if you supplement with synthetic fertilizers. But you're not replacing the organic matter, the biological life, the humus that makes soil functional. You're just keeping the chemistry going while the biology dies.

Composting is Howard's Law of Return made practical. It is the mechanism by which organic matter finds its way back.

## What Gets Returned, and What That Means

When you compost organic matter, food scraps, yard waste, animal manure, crop residue, you're not just returning the raw material. You're returning it in a transformed state. The thermophilic bacteria have converted complex, raw organic compounds into stable humus. Nitrogen that was volatile and prone to escape has been captured and stabilized into organic forms that plants and soil microbes can access slowly and steadily. Carbon has been built into the soil organic matter fraction that improves water-holding capacity, aggregation, and aeration.

You're also returning biology. Finished compost isn't sterile. It contains billions of microorganisms, bacteria, fungi, protozoa, that colonize your soil and become part of the soil food web. That living component is irreplaceable by any synthetic substitute.

This is the difference between recycling and return. Recycling recovers value. Return restores a living system.

## What Happens When We Don't Return

The EPA estimates that food waste makes up about 24 percent of landfill material. When organic matter goes to a landfill, it decomposes anaerobically, without oxygen. Anaerobic decomposition of organic waste produces methane, a greenhouse gas roughly 80 times more potent than CO2 over a 20-year window. The organic carbon is lost to the atmosphere. The nutrients are sequestered in landfill mass where no living soil can access them. The loop is broken.

Gabe Brown describes a similar problem in conventional agriculture, where crop residue is burned or incorporated without composting, organic matter percentages in soils drop steadily over decades, and the land becomes dependent on higher and higher inputs just to maintain yields. The soil is being mined.

Composting is the correction. It diverts organic waste from the landfill, converts it into soil amendment, and returns the carbon, nitrogen, and biological life to the land where it came from.

## My Personal Mission

I've said this many times: my whole goal is to create a world where we divert organic waste back to the fields, back to organic soil. Not just because it makes good compost, though it does, but because it would fix most of our unsustainable agricultural and ecological problems if we did it at scale.

Fix the waste problem. Fix the carbon cycle. Fix the water cycle. Fix soil health. Fix the nutritional density of the food supply. These things are all connected. They all trace back to whether organic matter is being returned to the land or thrown away.

I pick up pumpkins from farmers markets after the season ends. I collect food waste and run it through hot composting at the farm. The material that came from soil, that was grown from soil nutrients, fed the farmers market community, and then became "waste", goes back to the soil. The loop closes.

That's the Law of Return. That's recycling at its truest. Not just recovering material, but restoring a cycle.

## Composting vs. Other Organic Recycling

There are other forms of organic recycling worth knowing about. Anaerobic digestion processes organic waste in oxygen-free conditions to produce biogas and digestate, a nutrient-rich liquid. It's useful at scale for certain feedstocks, especially high-moisture materials.

Vermicomposting uses worms to convert organic matter into worm castings, a particularly biology-rich form of compost that works especially well for fine-textured materials and at small scales.

All of these are forms of organic matter return. All of them are better than a landfill. But hot aerobic composting, done at the right scale with the right feedstocks, produces the most biologically active, nutritionally complete soil amendment, and it's the closest to what nature does in a forest or a grassland without any human intervention at all.

The decay cycle is not waste management. It's the foundation of everything that grows.

## Sources

- Composting, US EPA: https://www.epa.gov/sustainable-management-food/composting
- How Does Composting Affect Soil Carbon Sequestration?, Sustainability Directory: https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/question/how-does-composting-affect-soil-carbon-sequestration/
- Assessing the Climate Change Mitigation Potential from Food Waste Composting, Scientific Reports, Nature: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-023-34174-z
- Closed-Loop Composting Systems, Grow Organic: https://www.groworganic.com/blogs/articles/closed-loop-composting-systems
